14: The ʿam hāʾāreṣ (people of the land) who stand at the coronation of the young Joash — wᵉhinnēh hammelek ʿōmēḏ ʿal hāʿammûḏ kammišpāṭ wᵉhaśśārîm wᵉhaḥăṣōṣᵉrôt ʿal hammelek wᵉkol ʿam hāʾāreṣ śāmēaḥ wᵉtōqēaʿ baḥăṣōṣᵉrôt — are the political force that restores the Davidic dynasty after Athaliah’s six-year usurpation. The ʿam hāʾāreṣ in Kings is not a sociological term for the rural poor (a post-exilic usage) but a constitutional term for the full covenant community of Judah acting as a political collective: they enthrone kings (2 Kgs 21:24; 23:30), execute traitors (11:20), and serve as the legitimate political expression of the covenant people distinct from the court and the military. The term anticipates what the NT calls laos (people of God): the assembled covenant community whose acclamation and participation constitutes the legitimate acknowledgment of the covenant king. The coronation scene — the hidden king revealed, the usurper slain, the people rejoicing at the restoration of the legitimate son of David — is the structural type of the resurrection-enthronement narrative: the Son of David hidden (in the tomb), the usurper’s power broken, the laos of the new covenant acclaiming the risen king (Acts 2:36: asphaleōs oun ginōsketō pas oikos Israēl hoti kai kyrion kai Christon auton ho theos epoiēsen).
1:
v1 — Athaliah destroys all the royal offspring
When Athaliah the mother of Ahaziah (em Achazyahu) saw that her son was dead, she arose and destroyed (va-taved) all the royal offspring (et kol-zera ha-mamlakhah). Athaliah, daughter of Ahab and Jezebel (or Omri — the texts vary), was the Phoenician-Baal-influenced queen mother who seizes power at Ahaziah's death. The word taved (she destroyed/she arose to destroy) is emphatic and total — she attempts to liquidate the entire Davidic line in one act. The motive is power; the method is massacre.
2:
v2 — Jehosheba hides Joash in the bedchamber
Jehosheba (Yehosheva), daughter of King Joram and sister of Ahaziah (thus Athaliah's step-daughter), takes Joash (Yoash) son of Ahaziah and steals him away from among the king's sons who are being slain. She puts him and his nurse in the bedchamber (cheder ha-mittot — lit. the room of beds/mattresses). They hide him from Athaliah so he is not killed. Jehosheba's act is the pivot on which the entire Davidic promise hangs: one child hidden in a storage room keeps the line alive.
3:
v3 — Six years hidden in the house of the LORD
He was with her, hidden in the house of the LORD (chuva itto beit YHWH) for six years, while Athaliah reigned over the land. The temple becomes the sanctuary — literally, the sacred space where the heir is preserved while a usurper rules. The temple personnel (priests and Levites) become the custodians of Davidic continuity. Six years is a long time for a secret to hold; the narrative implies that the temple staff's discretion and loyalty protected the child.
4:
v4 — Jehoiada summons the commanders and makes a covenant in the temple
In the seventh year (ba-shanah ha-shevi'it), Jehoiada the priest (ha-kohen) sends and summons the captains of the hundreds (sarei ha-me'ot) — both Carites (ha-Kari) and guards (ha-ratzim) — and brings them into the house of the LORD. He makes a covenant with them and administers an oath, showing them the king's son. The six-year wait is over; the priest-organized military coup begins in the temple itself, not the palace. The sacred space is the operational center of regime change.
5:
v5 — A third of you shall guard the king's house on the sabbath
Jehoiada instructs the commanders: a third of those coming on duty on the sabbath shall guard the royal palace, a third the gate of Sur, a third the gate behind the guards. This coordinates temple guards and palace guards into a unified security operation around the sabbath shift change — a moment when personnel movements would be expected and less suspicious. The sabbath rotation provided natural cover for an unusually large gathering of armed men.
6:
v6 — The gate of Sur and the guard gate
A third shall be at the gate of Sur and a third at the gate behind the guards. They are to guard the house (mishmar ha-bayit) so it is not broken through (min-hapharetz). The geography of the gates is partially unclear — 'the gate of Sur' does not appear elsewhere, and exact locations are uncertain. The tactical goal is perimeter control: all major entry points to the palace-temple complex are covered by loyal forces before the public coronation.
7:
v7 — The two divisions off duty shall guard the king
The two groups coming off sabbath duty are to guard the house of the LORD around the king. Jehoiada is staging the coronation with military precision: on-duty guards hold the perimeter; off-duty guards form the king's honor guard. The result is a complete military cordon around the young king from before the moment of public disclosure.
8:
v8 — Anyone who approaches the ranks shall be killed
The standing order: 'Ve-ha-ba el-ha-sederot yumat' — 'Whoever approaches within the ranks shall be put to death.' Anyone who tries to interfere with the formation is to be killed on the spot. The guards are to accompany the king wherever he goes. The phrase emphasizes that this is now a military operation, not a religious ceremony — the king's safety requires lethal force authorization.
9:
v9 — The commanders do as Jehoiada commanded
The commanders of the hundreds do everything as Jehoiada the priest commanded. They take their men — those coming on duty on the sabbath and those going off duty — and come to Jehoiada the priest. The obedience is total and rapid: Jehoiada commands; the military follows. The priest's authority in this moment exceeds the palace's authority, because the temple holds the legitimate king.
10:
v10 — Jehoiada gives the commanders the spears and shields of King David
The priest gives the commanders the spears and shields (ha-chanit ve-ha-maginot) that had belonged to King David, which were in the house of the LORD. The weapons of David stored in the temple carry symbolic weight: they connect this coronation to the original Davidic monarchy. Arming the coup with David's own weapons is a statement that what is happening is Davidic restoration, not a new political venture.
11:
v11 — Guards standing with weapons from south to north corner of the altar
The guards stand each with his weapons in hand — from the south side of the house to the north side, before the altar and the house — surrounding the king on all sides. The formation is described in terms of the temple's liturgical geography: the altar as the center point, guards forming a ring from one end to the other. The sacred space becomes a parade ground; the coronation will happen within the cultic precinct itself.
12:
v12 — The nezer and the edut: 'Long live the king!'
Jehoiada brings out the king's son and places on him the nezer (crown/diadem) and the edut (testimony/covenant document). The nezer is the royal crown or diadem — also used of the high priest's golden plate (Exod 29:6). The edut is more debated: it may be the covenant document the king was supposed to hold (Deut 17:18-19 — the king was to write a copy of the Torah), or a specific treaty or accession document. Then they clap hands (va-yikefu kapayim) and shout 'Yechi ha-melech' — 'Long live the king!'
13:
v13 — Athaliah hears the noise of the guard and the people and goes to the temple
Athaliah hears the noise of the people (qol ha-am ha-rats — the sound of the running people) and comes into the house of the LORD to the people. She had been in the palace, presumably unaware until the shout and commotion penetrated the walls. Her coming to the temple is the trap closing: she walks toward the sound without yet knowing what has happened.
15:
v15 — Take her outside the ranks and kill anyone who follows her
Jehoiada commands the officers: 'Bring her out between the ranks (bein ha-sederot), and put to the sword anyone who follows her.' His instruction: 'Do not put her to death in the house of the LORD.' The temple must not be defiled by blood — even the blood of a tyrant. Athaliah is led out through the horses' entrance of the palace and killed there. The killing happens outside sacred space, preserving the temple's purity while accomplishing the political necessity.
16:
v16 — She is killed at the king's palace
Athaliah is led out by force through the horses' gate (sha'ar ha-susim — the entrance to the stables and palace grounds) and put to death there. The location — a utilitarian, non-sacred space — contrasts with the royal and sacred character of the coronation just completed. The usurper dies at the horses' gate; the king stands at the pillar of the temple. The spatial contrast reinforces the legitimacy contrast.
17:
v17 — Jehoiada makes the covenant between LORD, king, and people
Jehoiada makes the covenant (va-yikrot ha-berit) — between the LORD and the king and the people, that they should be the LORD's people — and between the king and the people. This three-party covenant (God / king / people) is the constitutional foundation of the restored Davidic monarchy. The structure mirrors Mosaic covenant theology: YHWH as sovereign, king as covenant administrator, people as covenant recipients. The ceremony re-establishes what Athaliah's Baal-influenced reign had disrupted.
18:
v18 — All the people go to the house of Baal and tear it down
All the people of the land go to the house of Baal and tear it down (va-yitsetzu). His altars and images are smashed completely (shibber shibber — emphatic reduplication: they utterly smash), and Mattan the priest of Baal is killed before the altars. The pattern mirrors Jehu's purge in the north: covenant renewal immediately followed by anti-Baal action. The Baal temple in Jerusalem — apparently established under Athaliah who imported her parents' religion — is now destroyed.
19:
v19 — The king is brought from the temple to the palace through the gate of the guards
Jehoiada takes the captains, the Carites, the guards, and all the people of the land, and they escort the king from the house of the LORD through the gate of the guards (sha'ar ha-ratzim) to the king's house. The processional route — from temple to palace through the guard gate — is the inverse of Athaliah's exit through the horse gate. The legitimate king moves from the sacred space that sheltered him to the royal seat that is now his by right.
20:
v20 — All the people of the land rejoiced, and the city was quiet
'Va-yismach kol-am ha-aretz ve-ha-ir shaqatah' — 'All the people of the land rejoiced, and the city was quiet.' The contrast with v13 (when Athaliah heard the noise — qol) is deliberate: what was tumult becomes peace; what was an alarm becomes jubilation. Ha-ir shaqatah (the city was quiet) uses the same root as shalom — the city now has the peace it could not have under Athaliah's illegitimate and foreign-religious reign.
21:
v21 — Joash was seven years old when he began to reign
'Ben-sheva shanim Yehoash be-molkho' — 'Joash was seven years old when he began to reign.' The youngest king in the narrative of Kings begins a 40-year reign (2 Kings 12:1). His youth and the circumstances of his preservation — hidden by a princess, sheltered in the temple, crowned by a priest — are unique in the history of the monarchy. The Chronicler's parallel account (2 Chr 24) develops the story of his reign at greater length, including the sad decline after Jehoiada's death. For now, at seven years old, the Davidic line has survived.
